- In 2009, Net Absorption was at its lowest, approximately 20, while Completions were approximately 50, and Vacancy was near its peak at approximately 12–13%.
- From 2010–2012, Net Absorption rose from approximately 25 to 51; Completions first decreased and then increased.
- In 2013, Net Absorption was approximately 40, lower than 2012; Completions were approximately 30.
- In 2014–2015, Net Absorption was 66 in both years, significantly higher than previous years; Completions were approximately 50 and 60, respectively.
- In 2016E, Net Absorption is highest at approximately 70, and Completions are slightly below 70.
- The Vacancy rate has continuously declined since the 2009 high point, reaching approximately 6% by 2016E.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- In the time series, the Vacancy rate curve first rose and then continuously declined, forming a single-peak structure.
- The height of the Completions and Net Absorption bars shows a clear disparity across different years; sometimes Net Absorption is lower than Completions, and sometimes it is close to or slightly higher.
- The bar heights around 2009 are significantly lower than those at the beginning and end of the series, presenting a mid-period trough.
- Post-2014, the Net Absorption bar height is relatively high across the entire series, and the gap with Completions narrowed.
- Compared to the previous year, both types of bars slightly increased in the 2016E area, and the line continued slightly downward.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: In recent years, demand's capacity to absorb supply has strengthened, and vacant space has gradually decreased.

Business Implications:
- Market utilization rate has improved, and idle resources have decreased.
- The pressure to dispose of (lease up) new projects has relatively eased.

[Chart Components]
- The chart type is a time-series line graph with a single coordinate system, titled “OIL PRODUCTION FORECAST / U.S. Oil & Condensate Forecast February 2016”.
- The horizontal axis represents time, scaled from Jan-14 to Jan-21, marked by year.
- The vertical axis represents production volume, measured in MMBPD, with the same scale on both the left and right sides, ranging from 5–10.
- Two green curves are used: the solid line represents the “Base Case (439 oil rigs)”, and the dashed line represents the “-25% Oil Rigs”.

[Data Relationships]
- The Base Case was approximately 7.9 MMBPD around the beginning of 2014.
- This scenario reached a local peak around mid-2015, approximately 9.3–9.4 MMBPD.
- Subsequently, the Base Case gradually declined, approaching a local trough around late 2017 to early 2018, at approximately 7.8–7.9 MMBPD.
- The Base Case then rose again, reaching approximately 9.0–9.1 MMBPD by early 2021.
- The -25% Oil Rigs curve largely coincided with the Base Case before 2016, beginning to diverge around 9.0.
- In the reduced rigs scenario, production declined faster starting from 2016, reaching approximately 7.6 around mid-2017, lower than the Base Case.
- This scenario hovered around 6.9–7.0 MMBPD near 2019, followed by a slight recovery, reaching approximately 7.1 MMBPD by early 2021.
- By 2021, the difference between the two scenarios was approximately 1.9–2.0 MMBPD.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The solid line scenario shows a smooth curve of initial rise, subsequent decline, and then recovery, featuring a clear peak and a clear trough.
- Starting from the divergence point, the dashed line scenario is generally situated below the solid line, exhibiting a flatter shape and an extended period in the lower range.
- The two curves nearly overlap in the early period, gradually widen the gap in the middle period, and maintain a relatively stable differential in the later period.
- No sudden, sharp jumps are observed in the chart; changes are relatively continuous.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Under the reduced rigs scenario, the long-term production level is significantly lower than the base case.

Business Implications:
- The base case reflects a higher capacity for stable supply.
- Reducing rigs will weaken the overall output scale and utilization efficiency.
- The scenario difference highlights a high dependency on drilling investment.

Risk Concerns:
- If production falls below 7.0 MMBPD, it will undermine loading support for downstream facilities.
- If production remains 2.0 MMBPD below the base scenario long-term, it may lead to a contraction in contract fulfillment capacity.

[Data Relationships]

- Around Day 21, both groups are near the 100% level.
- Around Day 24, the blue line is near 80%, and the green line is near 30%.
- Around Day 27, the blue line rebounds to approximately 120%, and the green line approaches 0%.
- The blue and green lines remain above -50% throughout the entire period, showing no significant negative values.

- After treatment initiation, the original trend is replaced by two new curves, both showing a relatively consistent downward segment.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Both dosage regimens significantly attenuated the initial growth rate after intervention, with the higher dose group achieving a lower endpoint value.

Business Implications:
- Describes business status/issue/advantage: The overall burden expansion was significantly suppressed following the intervention.
- Describes impact on segment/metric/capability: Higher dosing intensity resulted in a greater magnitude of endpoint control.
- Further business inference: A positive correlation exists between dosage level and control efficacy, warranting careful consideration.

Risk Focus:
- Specific threshold + potential consequence: If the blue line persistently remains at ≥100%, control effectiveness is limited, and resource utilization efficiency is questioned.

- The right vertical axis represents the gold price, scaled from 800 to 2,000.
- There are two curves in the chart: orange represents Copper, yellow represents Gold, indicated by the legend in the upper right corner.

[Data Relationships]
- At the beginning of the period (mid-2012), the copper price was around 3.40, and the gold price was near 1,600–1,650.
- In the second half of 2012, the copper price briefly approached 3.80, while the gold price was slightly higher but changed relatively moderately.
- From early to mid-2013, the copper price rapidly fell from around 3.40 to below 3.00. Concurrently, the gold price dropped from approximately 1,650 towards 1,300.
- From mid-2013 to mid-2014, both prices generally fluctuated within the range of 3.00–3.30 for copper and 1,200–1,400 for gold.
- After 2014, the copper price generally traded below 3.00, briefly approaching a low near 2.00 at the end of 2015.
- The gold price was generally in the 1,100–1,300 range during 2014–2015, briefly approaching a relative low in the 1,050–1,100 area in mid-2015.
- Over the entire period, the copper price dropped from approximately 3.40–3.50 to about 2.00–2.20, representing a cumulative decline of approximately 35–40%.
- During the same period, the gold price fell from approximately 1,600+ to 1,050–1,150, a cumulative decline of about 25–35%.
- In multiple phases (such as late 2012, early 2013, and mid-2014), the directional changes of the two curves were broadly synchronized.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- Both time series exhibit an overall pattern of sustained decline, interspersed with multiple rounds of moderate rebounds and pullbacks.
- The volatility of the copper price curve is more significant; both the magnitude and pace of short-term fluctuations are noticeably higher than those of gold.
- The gold price curve is relatively smoother, fluctuating repeatedly within a narrowing range.
- In multiple phases, the two curves show clear periods of movement in the same direction, but there are segments where the trends are not entirely consistent.
- Towards the end of the sample period, the copper price curve reached the lowest area shown on the chart, while the gold price was in the mid-to-low portion of its own range.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Both copper and gold prices weakened significantly during the sample period, and copper exhibited higher price elasticity.

Business Implications:
- Indicates that the price sensitivity of base metal assets is higher than that of precious metals.
- Suggests that copper assets have a greater impact on overall portfolio valuation volatility.
- Further business inference: The higher the weighting of base metals, the more the net asset value (NAV) performance relies on a single commodity.

Risk Concerns:
- If the copper price approaches below 2.00 again, the net asset value (NAV) may experience an amplified bearish effect.
- If the gold price falls below 1,000, its buffering effect on the overall portfolio may be weakened.

[Chart Components]

- There are four curves in total: the solid black line is Control; the blue hollow squares are "ARQ 092 (50 mg/kg)"; the red solid triangles are "ARQ 092 (75 mg/kg)"; the orange solid triangles are "ARQ 092 (100 mg/kg)."
- Values "48%," "75%," and "78%" are marked on the right, corresponding to the endpoints of the three treatment groups at Day 21.

[Data Relationships]
- At the start (Day 0), the tumor volume of the four groups was similar, approximately around 100 mm³.
- At Day 21, the tumor volume of the control group was close to 2400–2500 mm³, representing the highest value.
- The 50 mg/kg group was approximately 1400–1500 mm³ at Day 21, significantly lower than the control group.
- The 75 mg/kg group was approximately 800–900 mm³ at Day 21.
- The 100 mg/kg group was approximately 750–850 mm³ at Day 21, slightly lower than the 75 mg/kg group.
- Mid-term (Day 14): the control group was about 1500 mm³; the 50 mg/kg group was about 650–700 mm³; and the 75 mg/kg and 100 mg/kg groups were about 300–400 mm³.
- The "48%," "75%," and "78%" on the right indicate the degree of difference for the three doses relative to the control group at Day 21, with the 75 mg/kg and 100 mg/kg groups being similar.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- All groups had similar initial values, followed by a clear divergence over the time dimension.
- The control group curve showed a continuous upward trend throughout the period, and the endpoint was significantly higher than the other three groups.
- Among the treatment groups, the 50 mg/kg curve is positioned in the middle, while the 75 mg/kg and 100 mg/kg curves are close to each other and generally occupy the lowest range.
- As time progresses, the distance between the groups gradually widens, especially between the control group and the two high-dose groups.
- At all time points, the curve changes for each group are smooth, with no sudden jumps; only the length of the error bars shows some variation.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: There is a significant difference in endpoint efficacy between the various dose treatment groups and the control group.

Business Implications:
- The chart shows that the treatment measure can significantly alter the level of the key outcome.
- The magnitude of outcome improvement varies across different doses, indicating a dose-response effect.
- The difference in results between the high doses is limited, suggesting that marginal improvement may be diminishing.

Risk Considerations:
- If the endpoint approaches the control group's approximately 2400 mm³, it implies insufficient intervention, potentially limiting therapeutic value.
- If the error range significantly widens, it may affect the reliable assessment of efficacy differences between various doses.

[Chart Components]
- The chart type is a line graph, titled "20DC New Container Price vs. Steel Input Cost."
- The horizontal axis represents time, labeled from Mar-05 to Mar-16, marked annually at the March scale.
- The vertical axis represents the price in USD, ranging from $0 to $3,500, with increments approximately every $500.
- The blue line represents the "20DC Price," the red line represents the "Steel Cost," and the legend is located below.

[Data Relationships]
- In early 2005, the 20DC price was approximately $2,300, and the steel cost was about $1,200.
- The 20DC price briefly approached a low of $1,500 in mid-2005, while the steel cost was around $800.
- The 20DC price reached a cyclical peak of approximately $2,600 around 2008, with steel costs simultaneously approaching $1,500.
- The 20DC price showed a significant peak of approximately $3,000 between 2010 and 2011, representing the highest level for the entire period.
- During the 2010-2011 peak period, steel costs were between approximately $1,400 and $1,500.
- Since 2011, the 20DC price generally declined from around $2,800 to approximately $1,300 by early 2016.
- Steel costs gradually decreased from approximately $1,400 around 2011, reaching a low of about $600 around 2015.
- Around 2016, steel costs showed a noticeable rebound from approximately $600 toward $900.
- Throughout the entire period, the 20DC price consistently remained significantly higher than the steel cost, with the price differential fluctuating roughly between $500 and $1,700.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The two curves generally exhibit synchronous directional changes throughout the period, indicating a strong positive correlation.
- The overall volatility of the 20DC price curve is greater than that of the steel cost, with more pronounced peaks and troughs.
- The 20DC price showed prominent peaks around 2009 and 2011, while the extreme values of steel costs were relatively moderate.
- After 2011, both curves generally exhibited a continuous downward trend, interspersed with brief localized rebounds.
- Around 2015, both were at cyclical lows, after which the steel cost was the first to show a clear upward inflection point.
- The price differential narrowed and widened across different phases; the spread was largest around 2010-2011 and approached the lower bound of the narrowing range around 2015.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Container prices are highly correlated with steel costs, but profit margins fluctuate significantly with the cycle.

Business Implications:
- Container prices are clearly driven by raw material costs, reflecting a strong cost dependency.
- Periods of widening price differentials are favorable for increasing gross profit per container and accelerating cash recovery.
- Periods of narrowing price differentials will weaken bargaining power and compress earnings elasticity.

Risk Concerns:
- If the price differential falls below approximately $500, it could lead to a significant contraction in profit margins.
- If steel costs approach $1,500 again without a corresponding increase in container prices, there is a risk of cost inversion (negative margins).

[Chart Components]

- There are 5 curves in total, indicated by the legend: NKTR‑181 100 mg (blue star), NKTR‑181 200 mg (red cross), NKTR‑181 400 mg (green diamond), Oxycodone HCl 40 mg (black hollow circle), Placebo (purple solid circle).

[Data Relationships]
- Oxycodone 40 mg reaches its peak value at approximately 1 hour, close to 75, followed by a continuous decline, approaching 0 at 12 hours.
- NKTR‑181 400 mg reaches its peak value at approximately 1–2 hours, around 15–17, significantly lower than the Oxycodone peak.
- The peak value for NKTR‑181 200 mg at approximately 1–2 hours is about 7–8.
- The peak value for NKTR‑181 100 mg at the same time point is about 5–6.
- The Placebo value is close to 0 throughout the entire duration, only slightly exceeding 0 between 1–2 hours.
- The values for the three NKTR‑181 doses in the 0–4 hour interval are ordered as 400 mg > 200 mg > 100 mg.
- After 8 hours, all NKTR‑181 doses are very close to the Placebo value, while Oxycodone remains above 0 but at a low level.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The Oxycodone curve exhibits a single-peak morphology, with a significantly high peak, followed by a gradual decline.
- The three NKTR‑181 curves have lower peak values and relatively flat peaks, followed by a slow decline.
- The three NKTR‑181 doses form an ordered hierarchical structure in the early phase, with higher doses maintaining a generally higher position.
- The Placebo curve closely follows the X-axis, showing minor fluctuations.
- There is no significant crossover between the different curves, indicating a stable ranking relationship.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: There are clear hierarchical differences in the subjective response intensity across different drugs and doses.

Business Implications:
- The subjective effect intensity of Oxycodone is far higher than that of other regimens.
- NKTR‑181 demonstrates a clear dose-dependent subjective response.
- The Placebo level is extremely low, providing a baseline for evaluating other regimens.

Risk Focus:
- When readings approach or exceed 70, the subjective effect is extremely strong, potentially leading to risks related to compliance and abuse.
- If future regimens at doses ≥400 mg approach the Oxycodone level, the safety margin and target population must be reassessed.

- The Warehouse component consistently constitutes the largest or joint-largest segment in each period, holding a dominant weight.
- Commercial and Industrial values were stable during the first four quarters, showing a single upward step change in the final period.
- Commercial Real Estate exhibited a relatively smooth, gradual amplification throughout the sample period.
- The structure among categories remained relatively stable starting from 2Q15, with only a slight adjustment in portfolio proportions observed in 1Q16.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The average commercial loan scale continues to expand, with the structure dominated by Warehouse loans.

Business Implications:
- Overall credit granting scale is rising, and asset deployment intensity is strengthening.
- The asset portfolio shows a high reliance on Warehouse and Real Estate loans.
- The C&I proportion is relatively small, leading to a more concentrated revenue and client structure.

Risk Concerns:

[Chart Components]
- The chart type is a line chart, with error bars, and a single vertical axis.

  - Blue circle: 500 mg QD, day 7
  - Green square: 350 mg QD, day 14
  - Green triangle: 250 mg QD, day 7
  - Orange diamond: 125 mg QD, day 7
  - Red inverted triangle: Japan cohort 250 mg QD, day 7

[Data Relationships]
- The 350 mg, 500 mg, and Japan 250 mg cohorts reached or exceeded approximately 85% around 1 hour, and subsequently maintained around 90% for most of the duration.
- The 250 mg cohort was slightly above 80% around 1 hour, then slightly increased before slowly declining above 80%.
- The Japan 250 mg cohort was very close to the 350 mg cohort early on, reaching its peak near 10 hours, slightly higher than the other high-dose cohorts, and remained above approximately 80% at 24 hours.
- The 500 mg cohort was slightly lower than the 350 mg cohort early on, but the overall difference was minor, with both stabilizing near 80% at the endpoint.
- The 125 mg cohort was approximately 40%–45% near 1 hour, rose to approximately 55%–60% near 2 hours, maintained the 60%–70% range between 3–6 hours, then gradually declined, approaching 30% at 24 hours.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The high-dose cohorts, including 350 mg, 500 mg, and Japan 250 mg, generally maintained a high level, with relatively flat curves, only slightly receding in the middle-to-late stages.
- The 250 mg cohort was slightly lower than the aforementioned high-dose cohorts, with both the starting and ending points positioned lower, exhibiting a pattern of gradual decline.
- The 125 mg cohort was significantly lower than the other cohorts overall; after a rapid initial ascent, it formed a mid-section plateau, followed by a stepwise decline.
- Clear stratification among the different cohorts was evident in the early stages, and the relative order remained largely stable thereafter, with no crossovers or inflection points observed.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The high-dose regimen significantly outperformed the low-dose regimen overall.

Business Implications:
- High-dose cohorts demonstrated a higher level of target achievement.
- High doses help maintain key metrics over a longer duration.
- The 125 mg regimen showed clear capacity deficiencies.

Risk Focus:
- If performance remains below 60% for an extended period, overall effectiveness may fail to meet targets.
- The 125 mg cohort approaching 30% at 24 hours may pose a significant risk of performance degradation.

[Chart Components]
- The chart type is a line chart, titled "Property-Liability Premium and Policy Growth."
- The vertical axis represents percentage variation (% var), ranging approximately from -4% to 10%.
- The horizontal axis is a time series, marked quarterly from 2012 Q1 to 2016 Q1.
- The blue line (with square markers) represents "Net Written Premium," and the red line (with diamond markers) represents "Policies in Force."
- Text in the upper right corner shows Q1 2016 relative to Q1 2015: Net Written Premium +$209M, Policies in Force -13K.

[Data Relationships]
- During 2012–2016, Net Written Premium maintained positive growth across all quarters, with a minimum of approximately 2.5% (2012 Q4) and a maximum of approximately 5.5% (2014 Q1).
- Policies in Force showed negative growth in 2012 Q4 and 2013 Q1, approximately -1.1% and -0.7% respectively, and fell back to 0.0% in 2016 Q1.
- For most of the period, the growth rate of Premium exceeded that of Policies in Force by approximately 2–3 percentage points or more.
- Premium rose from approximately 2.5% in 2013 to over 5%, maintained between approximately 4.8–5.5% in 2014, and subsequently declined quarter-over-quarter to approximately 2.9% by 2016 Q1.
- Policies in Force recovered quarter-over-quarter after the negative value in 2013 Q1, generally staying in the 2.0–2.6% range between 2014–2015, and then significantly declined after 2015 Q3 to 0.0% in 2016 Q1.
- Q1 2016 relative to Q1 2015: While the Premium growth rate declined, the absolute scale still increased by $209M, whereas the number of policies decreased by 13K.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The two lines generally show fluctuations in the same direction, but with different magnitudes; the blue line is at a higher level, and the red line is at a lower level.
- The Premium curve maintained a high plateau from 2013 to 2014, subsequently narrowing.
- Policies in Force experienced a brief trough in the early stage, then remained in a moderate range, and approached zero towards the end of the period.
- The vertical distance between the two lines remained relatively stable for most of the time, slightly contracting towards the end.
- In a few quarters, the red line approached zero or was negative, creating a clear divergence from the positive values of the blue line.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The sustained increase in premium volume significantly exceeds the growth in policy count, with both showing recent weakening.

Business Implications:
- Reflects that profit contribution relies more heavily on the output level per policy.
- Support for revenue volume relies more on pricing or structure rather than quantitative expansion.
- If the current pattern continues, business growth may become more concentrated in high-value policies.

Risk Concerns:
- If the Net Written Premium growth rate falls below 2%, the momentum for revenue expansion may significantly weaken.
- If the Policies in Force growth rate remains below 0% for an extended period, underwriting scale may experience substantial contraction.

- Following the peak, the index gradually retreated, remaining within the 4–6% range in 2012.

- The decline continued in 2015, reaching approximately -10% in Q4 15, close to the previous low point.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The overall pattern shows slight narrow fluctuations around the zero axis initially, followed by a deep decline, and then a strong rebound.
- The data spanned a significant numerical range between the lowest and highest points.
- The phase of retreat from the peak region was relatively smooth, without significant short-term counter-movements.
- The later negative range extended unilaterally quarter-by-quarter, approaching the earlier extreme low.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The level of lease yield experienced a significant contraction followed by a marked improvement, and subsequently weakened again.

Business Implications:

- The deep decline phase reflects pressure on lease yields, and profit elasticity was squeezed.
- The subsequent high-level range indicates a strong recovery in yields, providing significant support for revenue restoration.
- The latest phase, falling close to historical lows again, suggests insufficient sustainability in profit recovery.
- The entire cycle demonstrates that yields are highly sensitive to the external environment, with limited stability.

Risk Focus:

[Chart Components]
- The chart type is a line graph, titled "ARQ092 in KPL‑4 Tumor Model."
- The X-axis is "Days post treatment," with scales at 0, 2, 4, 6, 8.
- The Y-axis is "Tumor volume (mm³)," ranging approximately 0–300.
- The legend includes 6 curves:
  - Black open circle: Untreated control
  - Dark blue: 10 mg/kg
  - Light green: 20 mg/kg
  - Gray-blue: 40 mg/kg
  - Orange: 80 mg/kg
  - Red: 120 mg/kg
- Annotation: "Dosing: Q1D x 8," with error bars on each data point.

[Data Relationships]
- Initial tumor volume for all groups was approximately 100–110 mm³, showing minimal difference.
- On Day 8, the untreated control group was approximately 230 mm³, representing the maximum value.
- On Day 8, the 10 mg/kg group was approximately 210 mm³, slightly lower than the control.
- On Day 8, the 20 mg/kg group was approximately 150–160 mm³, significantly lower than the control and the 10 mg/kg group.
- On Day 8, the 40 mg/kg group was approximately 170–180 mm³, falling between the 10 mg/kg and 20 mg/kg groups.
- On Day 8, the 80 mg/kg group was approximately 80–90 mm³, slightly lower than the initial value.
- On Day 8, the 120 mg/kg group was approximately 40–50 mm³, significantly lower than the initial value and all other groups.
- By Day 2, tumor volume in the 80 mg/kg and 120 mg/kg groups had decreased to approximately 70–80 mm³, representing a significant reduction compared to baseline.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- All groups were relatively close at the starting point, after which their trajectories gradually diverged.
- The untreated control and 10 mg/kg groups generally moved toward a higher volume range.
- The 20 mg/kg and 40 mg/kg groups remained in the intermediate range; their terminal levels were close but slightly differentiated.
- The 80 mg/kg and 120 mg/kg groups were generally situated below all other curves, with the latter consistently being the lowest.
- The high-dose groups showed a relatively significant numerical decline early on and maintained a lower range later in the study.
- The curves generally exhibit a stratified structure based on dosage, with no significant crossovers observed.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Different dosages produce distinct, step-wise efficacy differences, with inhibition being more pronounced in the high-dose groups.

Business Implications:
- Indicates that the drug exhibits dose-dependent inhibitory effects in the current model.
- Provides direct guidance for dose design and efficacy balance.
- Offers quantitative basis for subsequent determination of the minimum effective dose and the optimal dose.

Risk Considerations:
- If the volume approaches the control group volume of approximately 230 mm³, it indicates insufficient inhibition and potential loss of therapeutic value.
- If the volume in the high-dose groups rebounds to >100 mm³ at subsequent time points, it may suggest that the continuous dosing strategy needs reassessment.

[Chart Components]
- The chart type is a time-series line graph, titled “Credit ratings over time.”
- The horizontal axis represents years, ranging from 2009 to 2015.
- The left vertical axis displays Moody’s rating grades, sequentially from top to bottom: Aa1, Aa3, A2, Baa1, Baa3, Ba2, B1, B3, Caa1.
- The right vertical axis displays S&P rating grades, from top to bottom: AA+, AA-, A, BBB+, BBB-, BB, B+, B-, CCC+, C.
- There are two lines: the light gray line represents “Moody’s: Baa3 | Stable,” and the dark gray line represents “S&P: BBB- | Stable.”
- A horizontal dashed line in the chart indicates the “Investment grade” position, corresponding to Moody’s Baa3 and S&P’s BBB-.

[Data Relationships]
- In early 2009, Moody’s and S&P ratings were near the Ba2/BB level.
- A significant downgrade occurred in mid-2009, with the lines briefly touching or approaching the lowest position near Caa1/C.
- From 2010 to 2013, the two lines remained relatively flat in the lower non-investment grade region, near the B3/B- to B1/B range.
- Around 2011, the Moody’s line was slightly higher than the S&P line, reflecting a marginally superior rating.
- Starting in 2014, the two lines began to move upward step-by-step, entering the range near Ba2/BB and B1/B+.
- In 2015, the two lines continued to move upward and approached or even reached the investment grade boundary: Moody’s approached Baa3, and S&P approached BBB-.
- Overall, the rating level at the end of 2015 was significantly higher than the level observed between 2009 and 2013.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The time series exhibits a phase of rapid initial decline followed by a long period of low-level lateral extension, succeeded by a structure of step-by-step upward movement.
- The middle segment remained within a relatively concentrated narrow range for several years, showing minimal volatility.
- The latter segment shows a stepwise upward progression, with almost every stage being higher than the preceding one.
- The Moody’s and S&P lines consistently run roughly parallel and closely track each other, with only minor grade differences observed in certain years.
- A clear single-point extreme low value appeared around mid-2009, and no similar extreme values have occurred since.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The entity’s credit quality, after experiencing a deep decline, has gradually recovered to near the edge of investment grade.

Business Implications:
- In the long term, solvency has shifted from weak to a neutral range.
- Recent credit improvement is conducive to financing costs trending toward reasonableness.
- For investors, the likelihood of inclusion in a broader investment universe has increased.

Risk Concerns:
- If the rating falls below Moody’s Ba2 or S&P BB again, it will restrict financing channels and raise interest rates.
- Failure to further upgrade above Baa3/BBB- may result in long-term stagnation in the non-investment grade category, impacting large institutional allocation size.

[Chart Components]
- The chart type is a cost curve line graph, titled "2015 vs 2014 Average Ethylene Production Cost Curve ($/ton)"
- The vertical axis represents production cost, unit $/ton, with a scale roughly from 0 to 1,200
- The horizontal axis represents cumulative capacity, unit Million Tons (Cumulative Capacity MM Tons), ranging approximately from 0 to 150
- There are two cost curves in the chart: gray represents 2014, and red represents 2015
- The red curve labels raw material and regional categories at several nodes, such as "M.E. Ethane," "N.A. LPG," "N.A. Ethane," "Asia LPG/Ethane," "Asia Naphtha," "Asia Coal," "W. Europe Naphtha," etc.
- The text "CPChem" and several connecting lines are marked on the left, pointing to several points in the low-cost segment of the curve

[Data Relationships]
- The 2014 curve is generally positioned above the 2015 curve, with costs higher by approximately $200–400/ton in most segments
- The starting point of the lowest cost segment in 2015 is close to $0/ton, subsequently reaching approximately $100–120/ton near a cumulative capacity of about 15 MM Tons (M.E. Ethane)
- At approximately 30 MM Tons (N.A. LPG) in 2015, the cost is about $150/ton
- In the range of approximately 50–60 MM Tons (N.A. Ethane to W. Europe LPG, M.E. LPG/Naphtha, N.A. Naphtha), the cost rapidly increases from about $170/ton to about $400/ton
- Near approximately 75 MM Tons (Rest of World, Asia LPG/Ethane), the cost is about $600/ton, marking a significant inflection point on the red curve
- As capacity increases from approximately 90 MM Tons to 120 MM Tons (Asia Naphtha, Asia Coal), costs are maintained at about $600–650/ton
- At the end of the curve, around 150 MM Tons (W. Europe Naphtha), the cost is about $750/ton, which is the highest cost point for 2015
- The corresponding high-cost segment for 2014 reaches approximately $1,000–1,100/ton at the same capacity level

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The 2015 curve is generally flatter in the low-capacity region and remains in the lower cost segment
- As capacity increases to the middle segment, the 2015 curve shows a significant steep rise, followed by entry into a prolonged high-level plateau
- The 2014 curve is generally located in a higher range, featuring a sharp increase in the middle segment, with costs remaining at a high level in the later segment
- The two-year curves maintain a roughly homologous structure across the entire capacity range, meaning the relative positions of the low-cost and high-cost regions on the horizontal axis are essentially consistent
- In the 2015 curve, some medium-to-high cost nodes show minor local fluctuations but do not form sharp jumps

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The overall cost of ethylene production in 2015 significantly decreased compared to 2014, with the cost differential spanning the entire capacity range.

Business Implications:
- The overall downward shift of the cost curve indicates a relative expansion of unit profit margin
- The relative advantage of low-cost facilities is maintained, contributing more significantly to the overall cost structure
- Pressure increases on medium-to-high cost capacity, and the competitiveness of marginal facilities weakens

Risk Focus:
- If costs approach the 2014 range of approximately $1,000/ton again, high-cost capacity may exit due to widespread losses
- If costs in the low-cost segment rise above $400/ton, the company's overall cost advantage will be significantly weakened

[Chart Components]
- The chart type is a grouped time-series bar chart. The Y-axis represents percentage, with 0% as the central axis, labeled "Surcharge" above and "Discount" below.
- The X-axis represents time points, totaling 6: March 2010, December 2014, February 2017, September 2018, December 2019, March 2022.
- Each time point includes two bar segments: the upper red segment represents the surcharge proportion, and the lower light green segment represents the discount proportion. Inside the green segment is a white dashed line, indicating a certain baseline.
- The Y-axis scale ranges from approximately -60% to 80%, with 20% intervals.

[Data Relationships]
- March 2010 only shows a discount, approximately -30%, with no surcharge proportion present.
- December 2014 shows a discount of approximately -10% and a surcharge of approximately +10%; their absolute values are roughly equivalent.
- February 2017 shows a discount of approximately -10% and a surcharge of approximately +15%; the surcharge absolute value is slightly greater than the discount.
- September 2018 shows the discount remaining at approximately -10% and the surcharge at approximately +20%; the surcharge absolute value is about twice the discount.
- December 2019 shows a discount of approximately -30% and a surcharge of approximately +40%; the surcharge absolute value is slightly greater than the discount.
- March 2022 shows a discount of approximately -50% and a surcharge of approximately +60%, representing the largest positive and negative amplitude combination in the chart.
- The green dashed line at each time point is located near the middle of the green bar and moves slightly downward over time.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The discount portion was initially large, then temporarily narrowed, and subsequently expanded significantly again.
- The surcharge portion evolved from non-existent to existent and continuously strengthened in the later period, showing a noticeable increase.
- In the later period, both the positive and negative ends of the bars significantly lengthened, widening the overall level gap.
- The length ratio of the upper and lower parts at different time points gradually shifted from being close to the upper end dominating.
- The dashed line remains relatively stable within the green area, only showing a slow downward movement.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The pricing system evolved from a single discount to coexisting discounts and surcharges, with the surcharge being dominant.

Business Implications:
- Reflects a more complex and multi-layered overall fee structure.
- Significantly increased requirements for flexibility and granularity in pricing decisions.
- Indicates intensified divergence in actual customer payment levels and more sensitive negotiation margins.

Risk Focus:
- When the surcharge proportion exceeds +60% and the discount is below -50%, it may lead to a noticeable decline in customer price acceptance.
- If the discount remains below -30% for a long time while the surcharge continues to rise, it may compress the profit buffer and amplify earnings volatility.

- From late 2021 through 2022, the interest rate rose significantly, peaking at the marked value of approximately 6.70%.
- Within the light yellow background area, the rate retreated from approximately 6.70% to about 6.42%, a decrease of approximately 0.28 percentage points.

- The latter section of the curve shows a significantly increased rate of change, forming a steep upward segment followed by a retreat.
- The end of the chart forms a narrowing range near the high point, without setting a new high or returning to previous lows.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The average long-term mortgage cost first compressed and then significantly increased, remaining in a high range recently.

Business Implications:

- Significantly amplifies the impact on housing financing burdens and monthly payment levels.
- Reflects the high sensitivity of the cost of capital to changes in market conditions.

Risk Concerns:

- If rates fall below 3.0% in the short term, it could induce excessive leverage and the accumulation of structured debt.

- The Investment proportion first rises and then converges over the long cycle; Household expenditure shows an inverse trend, with the disparity between the two being more noticeable around 2010.
- Investment was at a relatively low level around 2000 and a relatively high level around 2010; Net exports consistently maintain a low level.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Aggregate composition relies on investment, while consumption contribution is relatively weak.

Business Implications:
- Demand structure is more reliant on investment
- Consumption contribution to the aggregate total is relatively low
- Fiscal policy and foreign trade have limited impact on the structure

Risk Concerns:
- Investment proportion ≥50%: Consumption share is compressed, leading to excessive structural concentration
- Government + Net exports ≥25%: Dominated by policy and external demand, reducing flexibility

[Data Relationships]
- Actual CO2 Value: 2016 is 118, 2019 is 122, 2020 is 100, 2021 is 94, 2024E is 85, 2025E is 79, 2029E is 61, 2030E is 43.
- CO2 Cap Value: 2016 to 2019 is 130, 2020 is 100, 2021 to 2024 is 95, 2025 to 2029 is 81, 2030 is 42.
- Difference (Actual Value minus Cap Value): 2016 is −12, 2019 is −8, 2020 is 0, 2021 is −1, 2024 is −10, 2025 is −2, 2029 is −20, 2030 is +1.
- Total PV Market Volume: 2016 is 15.1, 2020 is 12.0, 2024E is 13.2, 2030E is 14.1.

- Change in Others Volume: 2016 is 14.6, 2020 is 9.1, 2024E is 5.6, 2030E is 0.5.

[Pattern Characteristics]

[Core Insights]

Business Implications:
- Compliance level primarily depends on electrification penetration.
- High sensitivity to the achievement of emission control targets.
- The marginal contribution of traditional powertrains significantly weakens.

Risk Focus:

- If 2030 EV+FCV < 7.2 mn, the difficulty of meeting the standard increases, leading to insufficient buffer.

[Chart Components]
- Type: Two data tables (Forecasts)
- Titles: BNP Paribas GDP growth forecasts; BNP Paribas consumer price inflation forecasts; Subtitle for both is "Developed Markets"
- Row Labels: Countries/Regions (US, Eurozone, Germany, France, Italy, Spain, China, Japan, UK)
- Column Labels:
  - GDP: ANNUAL GROWTH (% YoY, 2023/2024/2025); QUARTERLY GROWTH (% QoQ, 2023 Q1–Q4, 2024 Q1–Q4)
  - Inflation: ANNUAL RATE (% YoY, 2023/2024/2025); QUARTERLY RATE (% QoQ, 2023 Q1–Q4, 2024 Q1–Q4)
- Units: Percentage; No legend

[Data Relationships]
- Annual GDP Growth:
  - 2023: China 5.2% highest; US and Spain both 2.5%; Germany -0.1% lowest, UK 0.1%.
  - 2024: US 2.0%, Eurozone 0.7%, UK -0.1%, China 4.5%.
  - 2025: China 4.3% highest; Japan 0.9% lowest; The gap between China and Japan is 3.4 percentage points; Eurozone 1.6%, US and Germany both 1.4%, Spain 2.1%, UK 1.1%.

- Quarterly GDP Growth:
  - US 2023 Q3 was 1.2%; 2024 Q3 is 0.1%.
  - Japan 2023 Q1 was 1.1%, 2023 Q3 was -0.8%, a difference of 1.9 percentage points.
  - UK 2023 Q4 was -0.3%; 2024 Q4 is 0.2%.

- Annual Inflation Rate:
  - 2023: UK 7.4% highest; China 0.2% lowest, a difference of 7.2 percentage points.
  - 2025: Italy 2.5% highest; Spain 1.5% lowest, a difference of 1.0 percentage point.

- Examples of Quarterly Inflation Rate:
  - Germany 2023 Q2 was 6.9%.
  - UK 2023 Q2 was 8.4%; 2024 Q2 is 1.5%.
  - Eurozone 2024 Q4 is 1.9%.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- Annually, GDP growth shows a tiered distribution across markets, with some years being negative; the annual inflation rate had high dispersion in 2023, tightening its range by 2025.
- Quarterly, the GDP series shows multiple directional shifts and single-quarter changes; the quarterly inflation rate highlights local peaks in 2023, concentrating within a narrower range in 2024.
- Notable exceptions include Japan shifting from positive to negative within the year, and the UK shifting from negative to positive by year-end; UK quarterly inflation rapidly declines from high levels, while the Eurozone is at an intermediate level by the end of 2024.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: (Growth and price levels across countries are inconsistent, requiring segmented assessment)

Business Implications:
- Implication 1 (Growth leaders are not necessarily high-inflation economies)
- Implication 2 (2025 inflation rates are closer, making comparisons smoother)
- Implication 3 (Short-term decisions require strengthened quarterly tracking)

Risk Focus:
- Risk Point 1 (UK annual GDP growth ≤ 0%, lagging behind peer countries)
- Risk Point 2 (Japan's quarterly GDP ≤ -0.8%, potentially impacting its 2025 forecast of 0.9%)

  - Red Bar: Total Debt
  - Black Line: Debt/Equity

[Data Relationships]
- **Key Metrics and Changes:**
  - The peak value of Total Debt occurred in Q2FY23 and Q1FY24, approximately 23 INR bn.
  - The trough value of Total Debt occurred in Q4FY21, approximately 13 INR bn.
  - The peak value of Debt/Equity was in Q3FY22, approximately 0.8.
  - The trough value of Debt/Equity was in Q4FY21 and Q1FY22, close to 0.3.

- **Extremum Comparison:**
  - The change in the extremum of Total Debt is significant, increasing from a low of approximately 13 INR bn to a high of approximately 23 INR bn.
  - The difference between the minimum and maximum values of Debt/Equity is also notable, ranging from approximately 0.3 to 0.8.

- **Key Trends:**
  - Total Debt fluctuated downward between Q1FY21 and Q4FY21, then rose continuously, reaching a high point in Q2FY23.
  - Debt/Equity generally showed a volatile trend, declining after peaking in Q3FY22.
  - Overall, Total Debt and Debt/Equity exhibited similar fluctuation trends across several quarters.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The bars first decline then rise, forming a near-high level zone in FY22-23, followed by a pullback; the line rises from a low level to a single peak, then shows a step-wise decline.
- The two series are generally correlated (co-directional), but the timing of the peaks is staggered, with the line peak preceding the bar peak.
- Q4FY21 constitutes a common trough, and in Q3FY24, both are in a relatively lower phase.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Leverage exposure was relatively high in FY22-23, and capital headroom increased in FY24.

Business Implications:
- Debt accounts for a relatively high proportion of the capital structure
- Greater sensitivity to risk exposure and refinancing arrangements
- Increased available capacity in the FY24 phase facilitates structural optimization

Risk Concerns:
- Debt/Equity ≥ 0.8, potentially compressing new financing capacity
- Total Debt ≥ 23 INR bn, potentially increasing pressure on debt servicing arrangements

[Data Relationships]

  - Regarding Taxes, the Democratic sweep scenario involves increased taxes on high-income earners and corporations, and expanded tax credits for low-income earners; the Republican sweep scenario plans to lower corporate taxes.
  - Regarding the Fiscal Deficit, the Democratic sweep scenario might lead to a larger deficit, while the Republican sweep focuses on extending tax deadlines and scrutinizing large budget items.

- **Extreme Value Comparison:**
  - Regarding Tariffs, the Democratic sweep and Divided Congress, Democratic president scenarios maintain the status quo, while the Republican sweep scenario proposes a 10% tariff on all US imports, and potentially a 60% tariff on goods from China.

- **Significant Trends:**
  - Emphasis on Climate policy continues under the Democratic sweep scenario, while the Republican sweep scenario may relax climate regulations.
  - Regarding Foreign policy, the Democratic scenario maintains the geopolitical status quo, while the Republican scenario focuses on domestic affairs and cuts foreign aid.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The matrix presents the intersection of four scenarios and five themes, with content primarily qualitative, showing clear directional differences for the same theme across different scenarios.
- A small number of cells provide explicit thresholds, with values concentrated in tax rates and tariffs; the structure is decentralized rather than dominated by a single factor.
- The Republican sweep scenario shows the largest divergence from other scenarios regarding tariffs and regulation, while the Divided Congress scenario exhibits compromise and uncertainty in fiscal and foreign policy.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Different political paths will reshape tax burdens, import costs, and compliance requirements

Business Implications:
- Tax system differences dictate corporate cost structures
- Tariff levels impact cross-border procurement and pricing
- Regulatory orientation determines the pace of emissions reduction and projects

Risk Focus:
- Corporate tax rate of 28% or 15%; post-tax cash flow and pricing require recalculation
- Import tariffs of 10%/60% on China; procurement and supply strategies must be adjusted

  - The EU was approximately 125 TWh in 2022, increasing to approximately 165 TWh by 2026.
  - China was 225 TWh in 2022, significantly increasing to 300 TWh by 2026.
  - Denmark was approximately 5 TWh in 2022, reaching 9 TWh by 2026.
  - Ireland was approximately 6 TWh in 2022, rising to 12 TWh by 2026.

- Extremum Comparison:
  - China has the highest data point in 2026, reaching approximately 300 TWh.
  - Denmark has the lowest data point in 2022, at approximately 5 TWh.

- Key Trends:
  - Data for all regions in 2026 is significantly higher than in 2022, indicating overall demand growth.
  - Regarding the total demand proportion, Ireland is approximately 28% in 2026, noticeably higher than other countries.

[Pattern Characteristics]
Each region is presented with two paired bars, with the later period bar generally higher than the earlier period bar. Absolute magnitude stratification is clear: China is the highest, Denmark is the lowest; the proportion diamond marker is clearly prominent at Ireland. Structurally, there is a mismatch between volume and proportion: high-volume regions have lower proportions, while the low-volume group shows an exception with a high proportion.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Scale is concentrated in China and the US, proportion advantage is in Ireland.

Business Implications:
- Large-volume regions determine the main allocation direction.
- Ireland carries a higher weight in the total proportion.
- Strategies for scale versus proportion must be differentiated.

Risk Focus:
- Ireland proportion < 21% → Concentration decreases, weakening the pull on the total proportion.
- US volume < 275 TWh → Aggregate scale decreases, main market impact expands.

- The series oscillates around the 0-axis. In 2021, it initially lingered in the negative region, subsequently reaching a clear peak around 2022, then switching between positive and negative ranges, finally converging near 0 at the end of the period.
- The pink confidence band remains generally continuous, but its width significantly expanded during periods such as early 2022 and early 2023, indicating a phase of volatility range expansion. The range narrowed relatively during other periods.
- At individual points in time, the solid line touched or approached the edge of the confidence band, especially near local peaks and troughs, forming brief, extreme deviations.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The CTA's short-term directional exposure to USD exhibits phase-based switching and has returned to near neutral.

Business Implications:
- Indicates that the CTA's exposure to USD is not long-term unidirectional.
- The portfolio's currency sensitivity varies significantly across different phases.
- Current reliance on USD has significantly weakened compared to the 2022 peak.

Risk Concerns:
- If the beta approaches 2 or -1.2 again, it may amplify the portfolio's profit and loss amplitude (volatility) relative to USD fluctuations.
- If the confidence band width approaches the level seen in early 2022, it could lead to a significant increase in short-term drawdowns and performance volatility.

Risk Concerns:

The bars in the middle segment show greater fluctuation within the low range, while the final segment distributes around the segment average within a higher range, with volatility significantly narrowing. The global peak occurred in the early years, the trough was concentrated in the middle of the second segment. Although the final segment did not reach a new high, it did not revisit the low levels of the middle segment.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Gross margin experienced a significant step-down before recovering to a medium-high level.

Business Implications:
- Strong profit margin capacity existed in the early period, which was subsequently compressed temporarily.
- The middle segment showed high earnings elasticity, making it more sensitive to costs and pricing.
- In recent years, the earnings structure has been relatively contained, making volatility easier to manage.

Risk Focus:

- The initial value is approximately 0.6 lower than the Pre Covid Average

- The overall trend gradually rises from a lower level, interspersed with multiple rounds of step-like increases and pullbacks, operating near the sample high in the later period.
- A clear low point appeared around 2020, followed by fluctuations around the Post Covid average line, and repeatedly approaching or breaking through the +1 SD line.
- In the final period, there was a rapid short-term surge, with a significant deviation from the Post Covid average line and the +1 SD line. The duration spent in the high-level zone is still relatively short.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The valuation of the real estate sector is significantly higher than the post-pandemic normalized range.

Business Implications:

- The value proposition of this sector in asset allocation may decline.
- Subsequent returns are more sensitive to valuation digestion and fundamentals.

Risk Concerns:
- If the P/B ratio remains above 5.3 for a long period, the book value decline during a pullback may be significantly amplified.
- If the P/B ratio rapidly falls back to around 4.0, it may trigger short-term emotional pressure and redemptions.

- Road: Approximately 140 in 2018; Approximately 120 in 2020; Net increase from 1990 to 2020 is approximately 20 points.
- Maritime: Approximately 130 around 2010; Approximately 115 in 2020; Difference from Road in 2020 is approximately 5 points.
- Rail: Approximately 90 in 1990; Approximately 30 in 2020; Difference from 1990 to 2020 is approximately 60 points; Lowest among the four categories in 2020.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The four series converge near the starting point, then gradually diverge, forming one distinctly high position, one mid-level cluster, and one continuously declining structure.
- The Aviation curve shows an initial rise followed by a steep decline at the end. The distance between the other three curves narrows towards the end. Rail consistently remains far below the baseline and generally maintains a unidirectional change.
- Overall, there is no regular cyclical fluctuation; instead, the pattern is dominated by long-term gradual evolution, interspersed with a few short-term variations of significant magnitude.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The development of various transport modes is highly unbalanced, and their sensitivity to sudden shocks differs significantly.

Business Implications:
- Aviation exhibits higher expansiveness and stronger external dependence.
- Road and Maritime assume similar roles as fundamental transport capacity over the long term.
- Rail is relatively weakened, and its overall allocation weight has significantly decreased.

Risk Focus:
- When the Aviation index falls below 100 (such as in 2020), related capacity and revenue volatility may be severely amplified.
- The Rail index remaining below 50 long-term and continuing to decline may lead to further weakening of network coverage and service capability.

- Title: Not explicitly displayed; legend includes "JPY/g (y‑y)" and various countries/regions: Other, Hong Kong, US, Taiwan, Netherlands, Germany, China, South Korea, Thailand.
- X-axis: Monthly time series, ranging from 2007/01 to 2023/09.
- Y-axis: Percentage scale, ranging from -30% to 40%, including the 0% baseline.
- Annotation text on the right: "January: 21.6%".

[Data Relationships]
- The JPY/g (y‑y) line peaks at approximately 33% (near the end of 2022) and bottoms out at approximately -20% (mid-2016), representing a range of about 53 percentage points.
- The line value at the beginning of the period (2007/01) is approximately 8%–12%, and at the end of the period (2023/09) is approximately 18%–20%. The difference between the two is about 6%–12%.
- The annotation "January: 21.6%" indicates a monthly value of 21.6%.
- The overall height of the stacked bars is approximately -15% to -20% in mid-2016, and repeatedly reaches the 20%–30% range from mid-to-late 2022.
- The absolute value of the single-month bar values for most categories is within ±5%, with occasional months exceeding ±10%.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The line exhibits a clear structure of significant fluctuations across the entire period, showing a deep trough around 2016 and forming a relatively high plateau around 2022. The initial level is lower than the final level.
- The stacked bars frequently cross the 0% line. Negative values are primarily concentrated in periods such as 2008–2009 and 2012–2016, while values around 2022 are mostly large positive figures.
- Most country/region segments of the bars cluster around the neutral point. Extreme single-month performance is limited. The overall result is composed of multiple sources, with no single category maintaining long-term absolute dominance.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Overall Year-over-Year (YoY) performance has gradually shifted from weak in the early period to strong, with synergistic contributions from multiple countries amplifying volatility.

Business Implications:
- The magnitude of recent annual changes has significantly increased, reflecting enhanced profitability and demand elasticity.
- The regional structure is more diversified, reducing reliance on a single market.
- Both the positive pull and negative drag effects of extreme years on overall performance are increasing.

Risk Focus:
- When the overall performance falls below the -15% range, cash flow pressure and budget contraction may occur.
- When the overall performance breaks above the 30% vicinity and rapidly retreats, it is prone to triggering misjudgment of trends, leading to over-investment or excessive inventory stocking.

- The overall structure shows continuous, unidirectional change with no significant discontinuity, only a slight pullback in the final period relative to the preceding period.
- There is a clear contrast in duration and magnitude between the negative and positive value segments. 2Q24 formed the highest point of the entire series, with 3Q24 being a localized exception.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Gross margin movement shifted from significant pressure to substantial improvement.

Business Implications:
- Profitability quality was pressured in the early stages, followed by a clear recovery later.
- The contribution of pricing and cost control to gross margin gradually strengthened.
- The current profitability level provides strong support for subsequent performance.

Risk Focus:

- If the pullback exceeds 200 bps, it could significantly drag down profit growth.

During the Mar–Nov 23 period, both experienced multiple short-term rallies and pullbacks, with no significant prolonged consolidation in between. Starting in Dec 23, Price experienced a more rapid downward movement over a short period, diverging from the Rel Hang Seng Index, subsequently fluctuating slightly at a low level while maintaining the gap.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Individual stock performance consistently lagged the relevant index, and the divergence widened in the later period.

Business Implications:
- Relative return performance is lagging, resulting in higher opportunity costs during the period.
- Strong correlation with the index, indicating performance is largely driven by the overall market environment.
- Increased divergence in the later period reflects a growing impact of stock-specific characteristics.

Risk Focus:
- If Price breaks below 30 and remains persistently below the Rel Index, it may amplify relative drawdown pressure.
- If the Rel Hang Seng Index weakens again near 65, it could lead to simultaneous pressure on both the absolute and relative performance of the individual stock.

[Data Relationships]
*   Day -100: Blue ≈ -20, Green ≈ 0
*   Day -45: Blue ≈ -35, Green ≈ -20
*   Day -20: Blue ≈ +20, Green ≈ +25
*   Day 0: Both ≈ 0
*   Day +20: Green ≈ -60, Blue ≈ -10, Difference ≈ 50bp
*   Day +60: Blue peak ≈ +30, Green ≈ -40, Difference ≈ 70bp
*   Day +100: Blue ≈ +20, Green ≈ -30, Difference ≈ 50bp
*   Range: Blue approximately -40 to +30; Green approximately -60 to +25

[Pattern Characteristics]
The two curves are relatively close between Day -100 and Day -50, and gradually deviate above the zero axis around the orange vertical marker. Approaching the election day, both lines converge toward the zero axis and briefly align. After the election day, the blue line is generally above the zero axis and higher than the green line, while the green line remains below the zero axis for an extended period. The difference between the two remains large and relatively stable in the range of Day +20 to Day +100.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Brief convergence around the election, followed by a significant renewed divergence in the term structure.

Business Implications:
*   As the election approaches, the divergence in yield spreads across different maturities is significantly compressed.
*   After the election, the maturity spread widens again, reflecting differences in duration pricing.
*   The long-term segment spread remains consistently low, indicating higher sensitivity at the far end.

Risk Focus:
*   If the green line consistently falls below -60bp, it may imply a sharp price adjustment in the long-term segment.
*   If the blue line is above +30bp and the green line is below -40bp, losses from maturity mismatch may significantly amplify.

- The 2022 high point of ≈320 HKD is between the 20x line (≈250 HKD) and the 30x line (≈360 HKD)
- At the end of 2023, the red line was ≈180–200 HKD, between the 10x line (≈140 HKD) and the 20x line (≈280 HKD)

[Pattern Characteristics]
The red line fluctuated narrowly around the lower levels of the benchmark lines from 2014–2019, close to the lower boundary of the range. A steep ascent occurred after the end of 2020, forming a single distinct peak in 2022, followed by a retreat from the high and wide fluctuations between 10x and 20x. The overall structure shows a pattern of initial flatness, a sharp rise in the middle phase, and high-level consolidation after a pullback, forming a clear separation from the 30x and 40x benchmark lines in the later period.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The current valuation level is significantly below the high-multiple benchmarks, residing in the lower-middle range.

Business Implications:
- Compared to the 30x and 40x benchmarks, the current pricing shows a significant discount.
- Close to the 20x benchmark, indicating a downward shift in the valuation center.
- The retreat from the 2022 peak reflects insufficient sustainability at high levels.

Risk Concerns:
- If the price falls below the 10x line of approximately 140 HKD, it may trigger further emotional selling.
- If the price remains long-term below the 20x line of approximately 280 HKD, it may suppress refinancing and valuation recovery potential.

- Minimum Realised approximately -0.12% to -0.14%, corresponding Implied approximately 0% to -0.01% (green points).
- Maximum Implied approximately 0.08% to 0.10%, corresponding Realised approximately 0.08% to 0.10% (blue points).

Green points are more concentrated in the negative Implied and negative Realised regions, while blue points are more dispersed in the positive Implied and positive Realised regions. [Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The correlation between implied and realised skew exhibits systematic differences under varying spot conditions.

- Industry Series (Gray): Now approx. 8% (lowest); In 1 year approx. 10%; In 2-3 years approx. 20%; In 4-5 years approx. 28% (peak); In 6-10 years approx. 22%; More than 10 years approx. 10%. Range approx. 20 percentage points.
- Period-over-Period Comparison Difference (Red - Gray): Now approx. +2 percentage points; In 1 year approx. +4; In 2-3 years approx. +13; In 4-5 years approx. -7; In 6-10 years approx. -10; More than 10 years approx. -3.

[Pattern Characteristics]
- Both curves initially rise and then fall in the first half, forming distinct single peaks at different time points. The Company Series peak is earlier and sharper, while the Industry Series peak is slightly lagged and flatter.
- Both series have similar values in the early stages, show significant divergence in the middle stages, and converge again in the later stages, though both are below the mid-stage levels.
- A crossover occurs near the middle of the time axis. After the crossover, the Industry Series is generally higher than the Company Series, and the difference remains at a moderate level in the mid-to-late stages.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Enterprises judge the impact on themselves to be shorter-term, and the impact on the industry to be more mid-term.

Business Implications:
- Enterprises are more focused on the near-term operational impact of AI.
- The industry level is viewed as a mid-term restructuring and competitive landscape adjustment.
- The pace of internal transformation and external environmental changes is asynchronous in the short to medium term.

Risk Concerns:
- If the expected company impact exceeds 30% within 2-3 years while the industry impact is less than 25%, it may lead to insufficient preparation for external competition.
- If the expected industry impact exceeds 25% within 4-5 years while the company impact is below 20%, there may be a disconnect between transformation investment and industry changes.

- The sum of the proportions of the three categories in any given quarter is 100%

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The three inventory categories consistently form a stable three-tier structure across the entire period, but the relative proportions of Raw Materials and Finished Goods show a clear inverse relationship (trade-off) across different stages.
- Raw Materials were at a moderate level early on, then remained in a relatively high range during the middle-to-later stages; WIP generally stayed within a narrow range with minor fluctuations; Finished Goods relatively contracted in the middle stage and rose again in the later stage.
- Around 3Q21, the Raw Materials proportion reached a high point, corresponding to the Finished Goods proportion being in a low range, forming a clear structural extreme point. Subsequently, the structure slightly retreated and trended towards equilibrium.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: The inventory structure shifted from being dominated by Finished Goods to being dominated by Raw Materials.

Business Implications:
- The middle-to-later period relies more on upstream input rather than downstream backlog.
- The overall inventory turnover pace may be more driven by production planning.
- The stable WIP proportion indicates that the production process is relatively controllable.

Risk Concerns:
- When the Raw Materials proportion approaches 66%, weakened demand will amplify losses due to price fluctuations.
- If the Finished Goods proportion again approaches 50%, sluggish sales will increase inventory capital occupation.

[Chart Components]
- Type: A three-column, three-row scenario comparison table.

- Row Themes: 1) Macro and Policy/Growth Assumptions; 2) Valuation Multiples Level; 3) Relationship between Asian Earnings and Consensus Estimates.
- Color Scheme: Cells in the Base case column have a light-colored background, the rest have a white background.
- No axes, no scales, no legends, no overall title observed.

[Data Relationships]
- Years: 2023 (Peak Fed Rate); 2024 (Soft Landing/Growth Slowdown/Recovery); 24F, 25F (Earnings Forecasts).
- Valuation Multiples: Peak/Bull are above the historical average; Bear sees significant mid-year downward revision, year-end is "optically high" due to a low earnings base; Base is slightly below the average.
- Earnings Comparison: Peak/Bull are roughly consistent with consensus estimates; Bear is significantly below 24F and 25F; Base is below 24F and 25F.
- Macro Assumptions: Peak/Bull features moderating inflation, resilient growth, weakening USD; Bear features higher rates for longer, recession/stagflation concerns; Base features a soft landing.
- China and Semiconductors/AI: Peak/Bull is "stronger/continuation"; Bear is "disappointment"; Base is "cyclical recovery, continuation."

[Pattern Characteristics]
- The three columns of scenarios form a top-down matching combination across three main lines: Macro assumptions, valuation multiples, and earnings levels change progressively from optimistic to pessimistic according to the scenario.
- Within each scenario, the three rows exhibit consistent directional linkage descriptions, with no cross-contradictions.
- The Base column is highlighted with a light background, emphasizing it as the intermediate scenario, while the Peak/Bull and Bear columns on either side present contrasting comparisons in terms of macro, valuation, and earnings.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: Differences in the macro trajectory will directly dictate the landing points for Asian valuations and earnings.

Business Implications:
- Investment outcomes are highly dependent on the assessment of macro and policy trends.
- Valuation levels are constrained jointly by earnings revisions and risk premium.
- The performance of China and Semiconductors/AI will amplify return differentials between different scenarios.

Risk Focus:
- If actual earnings are significantly below 24F/25F consensus estimates, valuations may become passively high due to the low earnings base.
- If high interest rates are maintained long-term and growth is weaker than the Base assumption, portfolios will face the dual impact of valuation compression and profit downgrades.

[Chart Components]
- Type: Scatter plot, containing two types of points and two reference lines (blue dashed line, gray dashed line).
- Title: 5y Implied vs realised skew; last 3 months.
- X-axis: Implied skew, range approximately -0.20% to 0.20%, zero mark bolded.
- Y-axis: Realised skew, range approximately -0.30% to 0.40%, zero mark bolded.
- Legend: Blue diamond represents "Higher forward," green square represents "Lower forward."

[Data Relationships]
- Vertical Extremes: Highest realised skew approximately 0.35% (corresponding to x≈0.18%, blue); lowest approximately -0.25% (corresponding to x≈0.05%, blue).
- Horizontal Range: Implied skew scatter points are generally distributed between -0.12% and 0.18%.
- Series Value Ranges: Green approximately -0.12% to 0.03%; Blue approximately -0.02% to 0.18%.
- Relationship to Zero: Scatter points exist near both x=0 and y=0.
- Overlap Coverage: Both series have scatter points within the range x≈-0.02%—0.10% and y≈-0.10%—0.10%.

[Pattern Characteristics]
Both types of points exhibit a roughly linear distribution around the origin, forming an overall concentrated band structure in a single direction. Green points are mainly concentrated where the X-axis is slightly negative to slightly positive, and the Y-axis is slightly negative; blue points are more distributed in the positive X-axis and positive Y-axis regions, with a few outliers appearing at higher levels. The two types of points show significant overlap in the central range, but the higher value range is dominated by blue points.

[Core Insights]
Core Conclusion: In the case of higher forward rates, realised skew is relatively more dependent on the level of implied skew.

Business Implications:
- The indicative nature of the implied metric on future realised outcomes is more prominent under higher forward conditions.
- Under higher forward conditions, the linkage between pricing and realised results is tighter.
- Samples with lower forward rates have a relatively limited contribution to the overall relationship.

Risk Focus:
- When implied skew ＞ 0.15% and realised skew is significantly below the fitted line, it may lead to pricing drawdown.
- When implied skew ＜ -0.10% and realised skew is near 0 or positive, insufficient hedging effectiveness may occur.

The Transport series showed a slow increase around 2000—2010, forming a clear peak, followed by a brief, sharp drop around 2020 before recovering again. [Core Insights]

Business Implications:

- Buildings, Agriculture, and Waste contribute less but still possess room for continuous optimization.

Risk Concerns:

- If Public Electricity & Heat emissions rebound above 1000 MtCO2e, existing emission reduction achievements will be undermined.

- VI is at a higher level in this metric, possessing a relative advantage.
- Bharti maintains a median level, demonstrating greater resilience to peer changes.
- R‑Jio's significant decline may weaken its competitiveness in this metric.

Risk Focus:
- If R‑Jio consistently falls below 1.5%, it may affect its related revenue and bargaining power.
- If Bharti drops below 2.5%, its median advantage will disappear, and the competitive landscape may become significantly unfavorable.
